topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor’s cheerful pronouncements about the 'character' of the old Victorian house grated on my nerves. I stood in the cavernous living room, running a gloved hand along the peeling wallpaper, a faint tremor running through my fingers. It wasn't the decay that bothered me; it was the implication. Every shadow seemed to hold a stale memory, a residue of something ugly. I surveyed the room with a critical eye, cataloging every scuff mark, every misplaced object. The faint, almost invisible discoloration near the hearth made my jaw tighten. I told the agent I needed to see the structural reports immediately, my voice clipped and devoid of warmth. When the inspector finally pointed out the disturbed dust pattern near the fireplace, I simply nodded, a cold knot settling deep in my stomach. This wasn't charm; it was evidence.

[story 2]

I walked through the foyer, my posture stiff, every movement deliberate. The air in the hallway felt thick, heavy, as if someone had just breathed too deeply in the space. I kept my gaze fixed on the pristine, untouched surfaces—the gleaming hardwood floors, the perfectly aligned moldings—as if trying to filter out the underlying grime. My breath hitched slightly when I noticed the faint, almost imperceptible smear on the baseboard near the entryway. I didn't touch it. Instead, I circled the perimeter, my heels clicking sharply on the wood, a sound that felt unnecessarily loud in the silence. "So," I said to the agent, my tone flat, "this place has... history. A very messy one, apparently." I didn't wait for an answer, already mentally calculating the cost of remediation.

[story 3]

It was the smell that truly set my teeth on edge—a faint, metallic tang beneath the musty scent of aged wood and mildew. I moved through the kitchen, arms crossed tightly over my chest, refusing to let my shoulders slump in sympathy with the room's dilapidation. My eyes scanned the corners, searching for anything out of place, anything that suggested recent intrusion. There, on the bottom shelf of the pantry, was a small, almost invisible indentation in the wood paneling. A wave of profound annoyance washed over me. I pulled out my phone, not to call a contractor, but to take a high-resolution photograph of the spot. "This isn't charming wear and tear," I murmured to myself, the words sharp and dismissive. "This is documentation."
